Kernel Density Estimation in R

What ArcGIS Pro Hides From You

Kyle J. Redican, PhD

April 2026

Setup and Data

Load libraries and read in the Gainesville traffic accident data.

library(spatstat)
library(sf)

# Working Directory
setwd("C:/Users/kredican/Desktop/FloridaInterviewMaterials/KernelDensityMapping/Rcode")

# Load shapefiles
boundary <- st_read("GainesvilleBoundary.shp", quiet = TRUE)
accidents <- st_read("Trafficaccidents2025.shp", quiet = TRUE)

# Convert to spatstat point pattern object
win <- as.owin(boundary)
coords <- st_coordinates(accidents)
pp <- ppp(coords[,1], coords[,2], window = win)
plot(pp, main = "2025 Traffic Accidents, Gainesville FL", pch = 16, cex = 0.4)

Raw point pattern: 2025 Traffic Accidents in Gainesville, FL

KDE With Full Control

In ArcGIS Pro, the Kernel Density tool makes two decisions for you:

  • Kernel function: Quartic (locked, cannot change)
  • Bandwidth: Silverman’s rule of thumb (or manual entry)

In R, both are yours to choose.

kde_result <- density(pp,
  kernel = "gaussian",
  sigma  = bw.ppl(pp),
  dimyx  = c(256, 256))

plot(kde_result, main = "KDE: Gaussian Kernel, Cross-Validated Bandwidth")
plot(pp, add = TRUE, pch = 16, cex = 0.3, col = "black")

KDE with Gaussian kernel and cross-validated bandwidth

Bandwidth: The Most Important Parameter

Three different methods, three different answers. All are defensible. ArcGIS Pro only gives you one.

bw_scott <- mean(bw.scott(pp))
bw_cv        <- bw.ppl(pp)
bw_diggle    <- bw.diggle(pp)


bw_table <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Scott's Rule (normal reference)",
             "Likelihood Cross-Validation",
             "Diggle's Method"),
  Bandwidth_m = round(c(bw_scott, bw_cv, bw_diggle), 1),
  Notes = c("Fast, assumes unimodal distribution",
            "Data-driven, minimizes prediction error",
            "Minimizes mean squared error")
)

knitr::kable(bw_table, col.names = c("Method", "Bandwidth (m)", "Notes"),
             caption = "Bandwidth Selection: Same Data, Different Answers")
Bandwidth Selection: Same Data, Different Answers
Method Bandwidth (m) Notes
Scott’s Rule (normal reference) 848.7 Fast, assumes unimodal distribution
Likelihood Cross-Validation 139.5 Data-driven, minimizes prediction error
Diggle’s Method 4.9 Minimizes mean squared error

Visual Comparison

par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(2, 2, 3, 1))

kde_scott <- density(pp, kernel = "gaussian", sigma = bw_scott, dimyx = c(256, 256))
kde_cv   <- density(pp, kernel = "gaussian", sigma = bw_cv, dimyx = c(256, 256))
kde_dig  <- density(pp, kernel = "gaussian", sigma = bw_diggle, dimyx = c(256, 256))

plot(kde_scott, main = paste0("Scott (", round(bw_scott, 0), "m)"))
plot(kde_cv,   main = paste0("Cross-Validation (", round(bw_cv, 0), "m)"))
plot(kde_dig,  main = paste0("Diggle (", round(bw_diggle, 0), "m)"))

Same kernel (Gaussian), three different bandwidths

Key takeaway: The bandwidth you choose changes your conclusions. If your results shift dramatically with small bandwidth changes, your data may not support the analysis at that scale.

Kernel Shape: It Matters Less Than You Think

ArcGIS Pro locks you to the quartic kernel. R lets you compare. Here are four kernel functions applied to the same data with the same bandwidth.

par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(2, 2, 3, 1))

kde_gauss <- density(pp, kernel = "gaussian",      sigma = bw_cv, dimyx = c(256, 256))
kde_epan  <- density(pp, kernel = "epanechnikov",   sigma = bw_cv, dimyx = c(256, 256))
kde_quart <- density(pp, kernel = "quartic",        sigma = bw_cv, dimyx = c(256, 256))
kde_disc  <- density(pp, kernel = "disc",           sigma = bw_cv, dimyx = c(256, 256))

plot(kde_gauss, main = "Gaussian")
plot(kde_epan,  main = "Epanechnikov")
plot(kde_quart, main = "Quartic (Pro default)")
plot(kde_disc,  main = "Disc")

Same bandwidth (cross-validated), four different kernel functions

Notice how similar the outputs are. The kernel shape produces subtle differences. The bandwidth produces dramatic ones. This is why bandwidth selection matters more than kernel choice.

What Pro Gives You vs. What R Gives You

ArcGIS Pro R (spatstat)
Kernel function Quartic (locked) Gaussian, Epanechnikov, Quartic, Disc
Bandwidth selection Silverman or manual Silverman, Cross-Validation, Diggle, manual
Compare methods Run tool multiple times Side-by-side in one script
Reproducibility Screenshot the dialog Share the .Rmd file
Transparency Parameters hidden in tool Every decision visible in code

The Bottom Line

Pro is a great tool for applied GIS work. But when you need to defend your analytical choices, understand what the tool is doing, or compare methods systematically, you need to move beyond the GUI.

Every decision in this document is visible, reproducible, and yours.

Built with R Markdown. Source code and data available on GitHub.
